original_abstract:
Cyber-physical systems (CPS) are a cornerstone of modern infrastructure, yet their safety is frequently compromised by operational faults and anomalies. In this study, we evaluate the performance of open-source Large Language Models (LLMs)—including Mistral 7B, Llama 3.1 8B, and Gemma 2—in identifying anomalies across two distinct domains: battery management and powertrain systems. 

Our methodology employs a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) framework featuring a novel two-step process: models first infer underlying operational rules from normal behavior data and subsequently apply those rules to detect system faults. During experimentation, we observed that while standard prompt designs were effective for the battery dataset, they required refinement to handle the complexities of the powertrain dataset. By adjusting the prompts to emphasize explicit rule inference, we saw a marked improvement in detection accuracy. Empirical results show that Mistral 7B achieved F1-scores of 0.99, while Llama 3.1 8B and Gemma 2 reached a perfect F1-score of 1.0 in several complex scenarios. These findings highlight the critical role of prompt engineering and rule extraction in adapting LLMs for fault detection, ultimately enhancing the operational resilience of cyber-physical systems.
humanized_abstract:
Cyber-physical systems (CPS) underpin nearly all modern infrastructure, yet are more than frequently compromised in terms of their operational safety due to faults and anomalies. In this work, the suitability of open-source Large Language Models (LLMs) such as Mistral 7B, Llama 3.1 8B, and Gemma 2 for anomaly detection in two applications — battery management and powertrain systems — is investigated.

Our approach leverages a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) paradigm with an innovative two-step method: 1) the models extract operational rules from the normal behavior data, and 2) they apply the extracted rules to uncover system faults. Experiments showed that, while using typical prompts worked well for the battery dataset, fine-tuning the prompts was necessary for the powertrain dataset to achieve the desired performance. By fine-tuning the prompts to explicitly ask for the rule extraction, we achieved higher detection accuracy. The experimental results showed that Mistral 7B achieved an F1-score of 0.99, while the Llama 3.1 8B and Gemma 2 models achieved a perfect F1-score of 1.0 in several cases, which demonstrated the importance of prompt engineering and rule extraction for leveraging LLMs for fault detection and achieving the desired operational resilience for cyber-physical systems.

document.output:

Our approach leverages a retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) paradigm with an innovative two-step method: 1) the models extract operational rules from the normal behavior data, and 2) they apply the extracted rules to uncover system faults. Experiments showed that, while using typical prompts worked well for the battery dataset, fine-tuning the prompts was necessary for the powertrain dataset to achieve the desired performance. The experimental results showed that Mistral 7B achieved an F1-score of 0.99, while the Llama 3.1 8B and Gemma 2 models achieved a perfect F1-score of 1.0 in several cases, which demonstrated the importance of prompt engineering and rule extraction for leveraging LLMs for fault detection and achieving the desired operational resilience for cyber-physical systems.

Cyber-physical systems (CPS) are a cornerstone of modern infrastructure, yet their safety is frequently compromised by operational faults and anomalies. In this study, we evaluate the performance of open-source Large Language Models (LLMs)—including Mistral 7B, Llama 3.1 8B, and Gemma 2—in identifying anomalies across two distinct domains: battery management and powertrain systems.
